data.title: Workday: Positive Free Cash Flow Growth Ahead
data.description:
Summary

In my previous coverage on Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY), I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that WDAY AI's monetization strategy is gaining traction and that its growth outlook is well supported by a solid partnership strategy and the new WorkdayGo launch. This post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock.

The latest Analyst Day provided solid proof points that WDAY’s AI products, partner ecosystem, and new WorkdayGo launch are all scaling faster than expected. Taken together, these drivers suggest that WDAY is not only defending its core HCM business but is also unlocking entirely new growth vectors that should drive long-term FCF/share growth.

AI Monetization

In my previous write-up, I highlighted that AI monetization was starting to gain solid traction, and the disclosures at Analyst Day confirm that this thesis point has not only materialized but has surpassed my expectations. Management finally put hard numbers behind the AI narrative, revealing that WDAY’s portfolio of AI-powered products now generates >$450 million in annual recurring revenue (>5% of FY25 revenue), growing >50% y/y as of 2Q26. Clearly, this is already a sizable business that customers are actively paying for. Even better, management also broke out the newer “agentic AI” SKUs, like the Recruiting and Talent Mobility Agents, which have grown from <$50 million to >$150 million in ARR in just five quarters, representing growth of >200%. Management also quantified the direct top-line impact, stating these new agents are already contributing ~1.5 percentage points of ARR growth to the entire business. I don’t know about you, but this, to me, is the most concrete evidence yet that AI is accelerating growth across the platform.

With that context, I believe management made the right decision to double down on this momentum through acquisitions. I prefer buy vs. build here because time is of the essence, especially given how fast AI innovations are today. It's better for WDAY to acquire and get right into the game (i.e., have products quickly integrated and made available for clients) than to spend R&D dollars and time to build something that may not be as sophisticated. The acquisitions mentioned, Paradox (for ~$1 billion) and Sana (for ~$1.1 billion), are particularly noteworthy. Paradox is a conversational AI platform built for frontline worker recruiting, which represents a massive, underpenetrated market segment (makes up 70% of the global workforce). This acquisition is strategically important because it gives WDAY a specialized solution to target industries with large numbers of these workers, such as retail, healthcare, and hospitality. Through this, it also provides WDAY a valuable "landing point" to sell into new accounts, even those that are not currently using its core HCM products.

Sana, on the other hand, offers an AI-powered enterprise search and knowledge platform. Basically, Sana acts as a central knowledge hub, allowing employees to search for information and execute workflows across various platforms, not just WDAY’s. This acquisition is important for two reasons. First, it dramatically increases user engagement and platform stickiness, as Sana is a highly engaging front end, with users logging in an average of seven times per day. Secondly, it elevates WDAY’s position from a back-office system of record to the primary user interface for knowledge workers (again increasing stickiness and opening up doors to more “expansion”), which management intends to bring to all its 75 million users.

Another catalyst I foresee will accelerate WDAY’s AI adoption is the “Workday Flex Credits.” This consumption-based pricing model allows customers to allocate spending across AI and platform services. The apparent impact is this eliminates friction and ensures customers can scale usage without renegotiations. Once customers see the benefits, they will likely pay for these AI solutions, which basically help in monetization.

Growth levers

The partnership flywheel I expected is now clearly playing out very nicely, and there are new metrics that give more confidence. At the Analyst Day, management mentioned that partners continue to contribute >20% of new customer ACV (marking the 2nd consecutive quarter of >20% contribution). While two quarters don’t really form a trend, this is a very encouraging sign that the new go-to-market [GTM] model is resulting in a scalable growth channel. As I said before, as WDAY scales this partnership ecosystem up, growth should accelerate. I reiterate my view on this. The ecosystem itself has grown dramatically, doubling y/y to over 1,300 partners, including a fivefold increase in sales-focused partners. We should start to see the benefits of this expansion in due time, as this massive expansion extends WDAY’s reach into geographies and customer segments where a direct-only approach would have been too costly or too slow to scale.

The “WorkdayGo” offering, which is aimed at medium enterprises, has also seen solid adoption. As a recap, this is a pre-configured deployment that can be live in under 60 days (reduces adoption friction for SMEs). We now have tangible data to gain a better sense of how solid underlying adoption is—medium enterprise now accounts for more than 50% of new customer ACV. This very clearly signals that WDAY's decision to move downmarket and launch a competitive product is resonating deeply. Also, like I have said before, the addressable demand pool is massive, so this data point is really helpful in framing WDAY’s long-term growth potential.

At the same time, WDAY is also trying to penetrate the US federal government, which is a >$2 billion human capital management opportunity, with the launch of WDAY Government Cloud. This WGC initiative is important and should help WDAY win deals because it provides a standardized, scalable, and secure platform tailored to federal requirements (i.e., federal-specific requirements are directly integrated into WDAY), vs. costly and complex one-off custom projects. The key distinction is that productization makes federal requirements a standard part of the platform, while customization treats them as a unique, client-specific project. Put simply, WDAY’s strategy of "Productization—NOT Customization" should be more scalable and reliable and likely comes with a lower total cost of ownership for its government clients. The launch already shows signs of traction, with management noting strong engagement across the Department of Defense, the Intelligence Community, and civilian agencies. Solid traction here also opens doors for WDAY to penetrate other parts of the government budget (like state, local, and even international public-sector clients).

Taken together, the combination of a scaled partner ecosystem, strong mid-market adoption, and entry into federal paints a very solid growth outlook for WDAY.

Valuation/Guidance

Perhaps the most important development for the long-term investment case is the new financial framework. While bears are likely to focus on the softer subscription revenue CAGR target of 13-14% through FY28 (down from the previous ~15% target for FY27), I believe this is not the right metric to focus on. The important metric is the guide to deliver >20% Free Cash Flow [FCF] per share CAGR through FY28. This FCF/share guide seems achievable even with the 13-14% subscription revenue CAGR, as margins are expanding—adj. EBIT margin is guided for ~35% by FY28, a sharp increase from the previous 30% target for FY27. Also, management is buying back shares—they announced an accelerated $5 billion share repurchase authorization through FY27. This is a significant capital return program that will provide a meaningful tailwind to FCF per share growth.

With that, we can model out WDAY on an FCF basis. Management guided for FY26 FCF of ~$2.65 billion, or 21% y/y, and assuming that continues through FY28, I expect WDAY to generate $3.8 billion in FCF. For a business that is growing FCF at 20%/year, I don’t think the current 22x forward P/FCF multiple is expensive (implied ~4.5% FCF yield, which is just slightly above the US 10-year yield of 4.1%). I could argue that WDAY deserves to trade at a lower yield (i.e., a high multiple) to justify the growth, but even at 22x, I see a 30% upside.

Risk

Management lowered its subscription revenue target to a 13%-14% CAGR through FY28, and should growth continue to decelerate, it could mark a maturing large enterprise HCM market that is reaching saturation. This may shape up the bearish narrative that WDAY long-term FCF/share growth will decelerate meaningfully as well. While WDAY leans on AI to reaccelerate growth, execution is key, in that WDAY needs to make sure customers' concerns over data protection are well met. Any failure to do so here could trigger an exodus on WDAY offerings.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for WDAY remains a buy. The accelerating AI monetization, scaled partner flywheel, adoption in the medium enterprise market, and a push into federal create a compelling long-term growth story. Importantly, I believe WDAY can achieve the new guidance of >20% FCF/share CAGR through FY28 with support from a $5 billion buyback program. At 22x forward P/FCF, I see the current valuation as attractive relative to the growth outlook.

data.title: SharpLink: A Crypto Treasury Play Undermined By Weak Core Operations
data.description:
Opinions are divided when it comes to companies using crypto treasury strategies. On one side, there is enthusiasm, with some investors projecting returns of over 30x on their investment. Others think this is another bubble and that, in the case of another crypto downturn, these companies may suffer even more than the crypto assets they hold. I tend to be optimistic about crypto, more about Bitcoin than the others, but at the moment, I am not bullish on cryptos in general. When it comes to treasury strategies, for some companies like SharpLink (NASDAQ:SBET), I am also not convinced.

The treasury of a company is usually a store of cash to sustain its core business operations or for future strategic acquisitions, but in the end, the main goal of the treasury is to help the main business. If a company is not performing well, at one point, it will run out of funds, having to seek outside investment, through debt or equity, and both options could have a negative impact on investors.

In my view, a company holding crypto assets can be a good way to store value and protect from inflation over the long term, but only for profitable companies that don’t need to be seeking funds every so often because operations aren’t sound enough. To look at it from another perspective, if there are two companies, both executing a similar crypto treasury strategy, but only one of them is profitable in its core business. Which one would you invest in?

Well, of course there are different valuations, but the stock market is not that straightforward. Profitable companies usually end up trading at higher valuations than unprofitable ones. Also, more aggressive investors may use this to their advantage, going long on the profitable company while shorting the other one, which would give these investors a hedge in the case of a crash in the sector and may increase the difference in valuation between these two hypothetical companies.

In the SBET case, I don’t like it firstly because the company has never had a profitable year. If you look at the company’s finances, you will get the idea, but for example, SG&A costs in the last quarter were $2.3 million versus a gross profit of around $0.2 million. SG&A is more than 10 times greater than gross profit, and on a consistent basis. To me, that spells risk, no crypto treasury strategy will fix that, and in the future, the company may continue to lose money, requiring more equity financing, further diluting shareholders.

Another factor is the way the company is structuring its Ethereum holdings. As of today, it holds no debt. All Ethereum bought by SBET was from equity issuance, which diluted shareholders, and in the end, doesn’t create the asymmetrical gains that a company leveraged with debt can have during a crypto bull run, which most investors expect from a treasury strategy. For this strategy to work without debt, the company needs the stock to trade at a premium mNAV to issue more stocks, which is not the case now, according to the company's live dashboard the current mNAV is 0.98x, and there is no guarantee that it will trade at a premium in the future, given that other companies are pursuing the same strategy with Ethereum, like Bitmine Immersion (BMNR).

SharpLink's Business

Outside of the crypto strategy, SharpLink Gaming is an online marketing company that uses fan engagement tools, like quizzes and interactive widgets, to attract more paying customers to online sports betting sites and online casinos. They act as intermediaries and receive performance-based compensation when they successfully refer new customers, but also receive a commission when this customer spends money on games and bets. So, their main goal is to attract new paying customers to online casinos and sports betting sites.

The company has a history of losses despite being in the growing online gambling industry. That is mainly because of a significant SG&A expense, which, in my view, discourages the investment in the company.

In 2019, SG&A was $0.7 million, representing 29% of revenue, but in 2022, SG&A expense skyrocketed to $10.8 million, three times bigger than revenue, which was $3.5 million in 2022.

This sharp increase can be explained by changes in consumer privacy policy that happened in 2021, where Apple, other companies, and government agencies have implemented measures to stop unauthorized sharing of personal data online. Now, when accessing websites, users are explicitly asked whether they consent to cookies and other trackers collecting their data. This change has made it more challenging for marketing companies to target specific audiences, which, in turn, has driven up the cost of online marketing.

Another red flag of the business for me is that salaries and benefits have been consistently greater than the gross profit. This, combined with other expenses, has drained the company’s capital. At the end of 2023, its cash reserves were $2.4 million, and by 2024, it fell to $1.4 million, the business was running out of capital, and they were looking for options to keep operations going.

What could turn out to be positive is that after the acquisition of a 10% stake in a crypto casino. Now that the company’s valuation is tied to how much Ethereum it holds, this could help boost SBET’s valuation, although SBET has yet to generate meaningful revenue from this investment.

Risk

The biggest risk of the thesis is Ethereum prices, which also follow Bitcoin prices to some extent. Recently, I wrote an analysis on the Bitcoin cycle around the halving and the correlation with lower interest rates, explaining that the period of falling rates was actually detrimental to Bitcoin prices due to better opportunities in other less risky markets. I also outlined the lack of correlation with the M2 money supply during important downturns, and more. In the analysis, I explained that I am expecting lower Bitcoin prices starting around November and throughout 2026, but I am still waiting for confirmation. If this turns out to be the case, then Ethereum and the other cryptocurrencies may follow suit. Considering that, in the past, cryptocurrencies have dropped more than 80%, I think it is wise to be on the lookout for this scenario to happen.

Given that SBET is not profitable, and other companies are doing basically the same thing, but with stronger operational finances, like Bitmine Immersion (BMNR), cited earlier, there is a risk of SBET not being traded at a premium to its Ethereum holdings, thus not being able to execute its strategy properly. Yet, with SBET constantly losing money in its operations, the company may have to dilute shareholders even with a lower valuation in comparison with its Ethereum holdings, which would be detrimental to shareholders.

To simplify, the risks are that Ethereum prices may fall. The dependency of the company to be traded at a premium to mNAV in order to execute its strategy, which may not happen. Also, due to its core operations not being profitable, SBET may have to dilute shareholders below mNAV.

Conclusion

Investors are seeking ways to be exposed to crypto in a leveraged manner. However, that may not happen with SBET since there are better options in the market with better financial results from operations. Additionally, my view on cryptocurrency prices for next year is not positive, given the historical pattern around the cycle and better opportunities in other markets, with much less risk, arising from the period of lowering interest rates. For now, the stock is a Sell.

data.title: Brookfield Renewable: Trump's Policy Gambit Vs. Brookfield's Global Diversification
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The grass might seem greener for Brookfield Renewable Partners (NYSE:BEP) investors looking at high-yield stocks with double-digit yields.

I've been there, and I'm here to tell you, it's not.

Many high-yielding stocks operate an ice-cube business model, whose shortcomings inevitably translate to a decline in NAV and share price. In fact, entire sectors, such as Business Development Companies "BDCs" typically have high yields, but also NAV, dividend, and price per share declines. Their mandatory dividend distribution requirements mean that these entities can't build up loan loss reserves. Sometimes it's about leverage, as seen in the case of AGNC Investment Corp (AGNC). Sometimes it's risky assets, such as the case of Eagle Point Credit (ECC) and Oxford Lane (OXLC).

Brookfield Corporation (BN) and its publicly traded subsidiaries, such as BEP, are a different breed. Their mission, asset quality, and regulatory landscape are completely different. They emphasize stability, dividend growth, and long-term capital preservation.

I've done the math. Take Eagle Point Credit, which yields 24%. Between October 2015 and the present, for each 100 trading days, there were fewer than 13 days in which an investor could have bought shares and locked an annualized total return of 8% or higher. The vast majority of investors are sitting on annualized yields of 5% or lower. The same goes for Oxford Lane, AGNC, and many others.

The stability of BEP, and all of the companies under the BN umbrella, isn't something to scoff at. They're built to last through economic cycles, unlike many high-yield funds out there.

BEP now has a yield on cost of 5.78%. Even when ignoring its growth, it's already generating more than most high-yield tickers would generate in total returns, when including the share price declines in the equation.

The Brookfield Stack

BN is huge. It owns interests in oil & gas pipelines, seaports, road toll stations, real estate (including residential, commercial, and hospitality properties), data centers, communication towers, and utility companies (electric, gas, water, and waste). Additionally, it operates hydroelectric, solar, wind, and nuclear power plants.

To make it easier for investors to gain exposure to specific asset classes, BN spins off its subsidiaries into standalone public companies, while retaining a significant interest.

If you're looking to gain exposure to everything, buy BN.

BEP holds renewable energy assets. BN has a 48% ownership in BEP (p. 62).

Investors can gain access to BEP's portfolio either through its ticker (partnership units), or through exchangeable common equity shares (NYSE:BEPC) and (TSX:BEPC:CA), which are exchangeable 1:1 with BEP units.

Looking Past Accounting Noise

Although BEP reports IFRS losses, this is largely due to non-cash items, such as long-term hedging derivatives, which impact short-term performance, and inflated depreciation resulting from IFRS asset revaluation, a unique accounting method that allows companies to report assets at their fair value instead of their historical value. This means that depreciation no longer reflects historical costs, rendering it less useful in assessing performance. Revaluation accounting is particularly an issue for a company such as BEP, whose entire model is based on asset recycling: purchasing power generation assets, stabilizing them, and subsequently selling a portion to lock in capital gains.

H1 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019
Earnings per LP unit -$0.58 -$0.89 -$0.32 -$0.60 -$0.69 -$0.61 -$0.26

For this reason, management emphasizes Funds from Operations "FFO" in its presentations.

Examining the FFO/share chart below, we observe a strong upward trend. However, we also observe that FFO/share declined from 2014 to 2016. This period coincided with a drought in Brazil, which impacted hydroelectric power generation. The company also raised equity for the first time since its spin-off in 2011, adding to the pressure on FFO/share. The bottom line is, while BEP's operations are tied to contracted power supply agreements, often with inflation escalators, it is not immune to weather-related volatility. That's one factor that rating agencies consider when assigning BEP a BBB+ rating, one of the highest in the renewable infrastructure market.

Grid Bottlenecks Become an Opportunity

BEP currently enjoys an exceptionally strong outlook, thanks to surging demand for power, which has become the primary bottleneck in the buildout of AI infrastructure.

BEP has long supplied power to big tech companies, but the size of the commitments has become substantial. For example, Microsoft (MSFT) recently signed a framework for a potential 10.6 GW capacity over the next 5 years. For perspective, BEP's current proportionate capacity sits at 46 GW. Alphabet has also recently signed a 3 GW contract.

We view these developments as accommodative for BEP, allowing it to achieve its long-term goal of growing FFO/share by 10% annually.

Diversification Cushions Policy Shift

Trump's executive orders and the recent budget bill have created some tailwinds for the renewable market, but not as disruptive as many thought. For example, while Trump is phasing down support for wind and solar, current projects and those commencing construction before July 2026 are still eligible for tax credits.

Moreover, the big, beautiful bill naturally impacts assets in the United States. BEP is well diversified, with more than 43% of FFO derived from international markets, including Mexico and Canada, which are included in the North America segment.

Finally, the Trump administration is turning its back mainly on wind and solar, which are now established technologies, with little need for federal support to stand on their own. There is no significant impact on the federal support for hydro power and storage, which, combined, constitute 53% of BEP's FFO.

Baby Bonds Sitting on Contracted Assets

Some analysts have noted BEP's high debt load, which on an IFRS basis is reported as $37.7 billion (p. 2). However, we need to adjust this figure for the minority partners' portion of this debt in the same way we adjust FFO and adjusted EBITDA. All in all, BEP's portion of this debt is roughly $16.6 billion (p. 23). This includes various tranches of corporate and non-recourse debt, with a weighted average interest rate of 5.3%. This roughly translates to an annual interest expense of $886 million.

Note: In Q2 2025, management reported proportionate interest expense at $238 million, which, when annualized, is slightly above our interest expense run rate calculated above. The proportionate weighted average interest rate for non-recourse debt is reported as 5.6%, compared to 4.6% for corporate debt (p. 3). Our 5.3% estimate is calculated based on the interest rate schedule on p. 23 of the Q2 2025 quarterly filing, which includes both corporate and non-recourse debt.

Annualizing H1 2025 Adjusted EBITDA of $1.3 billion, we expect a proportionate interest rate coverage ratio of 3x, a comfortable place to be for (BEPH), (BEPI), and (BEPJ) baby bond holders, carrying a yield-on-cost of 6.9%, 7.0%, and 7.1% annually. The only drawback to these bonds is that they're perpetual, with no maturity date. Thus, they don't offer a clean exit route, exposing investors to the risk of changes in the market position of the underlying operations.

Dividends Payout Ratio Covered

In the six months ended June 2025, the company distributed $0.75 per unit (including BEPC), compared to $1.04 in proportionate FFO, resulting in an adjusted cash payout ratio of approximately 72%. This provides some cushion against fluctuations in FFO. Note: Proportionate FFO is net of interest expense and preferred distribution, offering us confidence in the payout ratio above.

Given that BEPC trades at a premium to BEP, it is worth considering the latter as an alternative.

Note: Investors might need to file additional tax forms related to holding BEP partnership units.

Final Thoughts and Risks

Many high-yield stocks appear attractive to income-oriented investors. However, after accounting for share price declines, most end up with total returns at the same level, or even lower, than those of investment-grade companies.

Despite the recent regulatory changes, we still see BEP as an attractive investment, with ~230 GW of pipeline projects, offering a multi-year growth runway for long-term investors. The momentum created by the buildup of AI data centers is translating into huge commitments from hyperscalers, as reflected in the recently announced deals.

Despite the reported IFRS losses, the interest on BEP's baby bonds is covered by profitable, cash-generating, power supply assets. The diversification across different asset classes and geographies adds to the allure of BEP and its baby bonds.

The Trump administration's detrimental stance towards wind and solar has not yet translated to a monumental change in the investment thesis. The situation remains fluid, but we remain bullish on all four BEPs: BEP, BEPH, BEPI, and BEPJ.

The primary risk is tied to power rates, economic and environmental cycles, which could have a significant impact on FFO, despite the contracted power supply agreements.

Overall, we see a positive risk/reward balance in this ticker.

data.title: Micron: HBM Is A Money Printing Machine
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) reported its Q4 FY25 earnings after the bell, confirming our belief that HBM has put Micron at the heart of the AI infrastructure boom. The stock's run-up to new all-time highs of $170 per share (now trading at $166) is more than well warranted, as fuel from AI demand continues to push the need for DRAM, specifically high-bandwidth memory ("HBM") solutions, for AI server build-outs, and allows for a higher ASP that’s pushing top-line growth and expanding margins. With at least $400B committed to AI data center build-out this month alone, we’re expecting more upside for Micron before this AI tailwind lets out.

This quarter, the combined revenue from Micron’s HBM, high-capacity DIMMs, and LP server DRAM reached $10B, meaning it grew fivefold from FY24. AI demand for HBM supported Micron’s almost 50% revenue growth for FY, and management raised its server growth outlook for CY2025, which tells us this isn’t the end of high-double-digit growth for Micron.

The stock is up about 31% since the last time it reported earnings, and recovered about 58% from its intra-quarter low of $104 per share. Ordinarily, such a run would make us turn more cautious, especially seeing the market’s lackluster reaction to what was an incredible print (with the stock only up a little over 1%), but Micron has a golden shield called HBM. The company holds the second-largest share within HBM, at about 21% according to Counterpoint.

In terms of DRAM market share, Micron remains in third place with about 25% market share after SK hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) in first place and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) in second. There is some chatter that Samsung is trying to climb back up the ladder and take more HBM share, which would mean de-throning Micron as second place. We don’t think investors should waste too much energy worrying about that, considering that:

  1. HBM's total addressable market, or TAM, is expanding. Earlier this year, management guided the HBM TAM to be $100B by 2030. A lot more AI infrastructure spend has been announced since then (whether in terms of raised CapEx from Meta Platforms (META) and Google (GOOGL), or OpenAI signing a $300B deal with Oracle (ORCL), or Nvidia (NVDA) committing $100B to build out AI data centers for OpenAI). Management still expects HBM CAGR to outpace DRAM CAGR.
  2. Now we’re seeing HBM transition to HBM3E and then HBM4 and then HBM4E, which should bring even better pricing dynamics. HBM3 pricing agreements are also completed with most customers to ensure the 2026 supply. So even while competition is intensifying, we think that means a wider addressable market for Micron, not a tighter opportunity.

The ASIC catalyst no one is talking about

Everyone is positive on Micron and its future within AI servers. We are, too. However, what makes us bullish even with the stock trading at around $166 per share is the next leg of growth, which we’re expecting to come from AI custom ASIC demand joining the HBM frenzy.

We talked about this in our last article on Micron and continue to emphasize that this is the catalyst that has yet to be priced in, considering that ASIC demand is expected to boom next year, meaning more AI ASIC will switch over from LPDDR/GDDR to HBM for higher memory content per chip. Current forecasts place HBM GPU demand growth at 23% Y/Y in 2026 and ASIC at 82% growth. This would mean that ASIC would go from making up 10% of HBM shipments to almost 33%. The chart below is from a JPM report earlier this year that estimates the added ASIC opportunity.

It’s all about pricing

The shortcut to track Micron is pricing dynamics; these are trackable for both DRAM and NAND, but our thesis is more centered on the former as the main growth engine, considering that NAND predominantly operates in smartphone and PC end markets, where demand is still lacking this year. Not to mention that DRAM sales still make up the bulk of Micron’s revenues at 79% this quarter, up from 69% in a year ago quarter, and ahead of NAND at 20%.

Micron’s DRAM is in a whole other ballpark because of HBM and the pricing boost it offers. DRAM ASP bounced 12% sequentially this quarter, as shown below, with NAND ASP increasing about 8%. DRAM revenue was up 27% sequentially to $8.98B with higher cloud customer spend on AI infrastructure. Out of that, HBM sales grew 33% Q/Q to $2B, making up 22% of total DRAM sales, so $1.97B.

Gross Margins continue to recover handsomely, and HBM is to thank

The higher DRAM mix in sales is also showing up very handsomely on gross margins, with margins expanding 670 basis points this quarter to 45.7% on better pricing. For the next quarter, management is expecting margins to expand another 580 basis points to 51.5%. When asked on the call if margins could continue to expand, management noted that

“..on the supply side, customer inventory levels are healthy. Our supply is lean. Our DRAM inventories are below target. NAND continues to improve.”

Management is guiding sales to grow to $12.2B to $12.8B next quarter, versus the consensus for $11.8B.

Bit demand is on its way up, too

DRAM bit demand is expected to grow in the high-teens percentage in CY25, and an uptick in server unit growth is to thank as demand outpaces supply. The good news is that supply should continue to be tight in CY2026 with an anticipated bit growth of mid-teen CAGR for DRAM and NAND. Management is pouring in a lot of capital to keep its competitive edge against the likes of SK hynix and Samsung on the DRAM front, investing about $13.8B in CapEx for FY25 targeted towards 1γ DRAM and HBM-related investments. For FY26, management is expecting a 30% increase in CapEx spend to $18B focused on expanding DRAM capacity. The supply constraint shouldn’t be a point of concern for investors; instead, it should be a point of pride, considering that as more products transition to 1γ, the 1β capacity will be able to support higher HBM growth in 2026.

Bottom Line: The logic behind investing in Micron stock & its valuation

We’ve been bullish on Micron since the stock was hovering in $85 range back in December (since which it’s up about 95% versus S&P500 up 14%). We told investors to load up on shares in the high $60 range into the April lows, and it’s played out pretty well. Since our last article that focused on the HBM catalyst, which is now showing up on earnings, the stock is up 52%. What most investors don’t know about Micron is that over the past two quarters, we’ve seen HBM shows up on earnings; prior to that, we were just getting the benefit of recovering DRAM pricing dynamics after a rough post-COVID era. Now, we’re in the era of HBM, and MU is still the best positioned memory play.

Micron isn't being valued like an AI name, although it's trading like one. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 10.69x, about 58.47% below the sector median and well under its own five-year average of 76.11x. On EV/Sales, the stock trades at a forward EV/Sales of 3.60x, in line with the sector average of 3.53x. The company is far more attractive in terms of profitability metrics, with a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of just 7.16x, representing a 54% discount to the sector. Micron is more than fairly valued at current levels, in our opinion, particularly if the memory cycle turns as expected, which pricing tells us it will.

data.title: Is The U.S. Dollar At A Crossroads?
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

In 2025, the U.S. dollar's image of unassailable strength is being rigorously questioned. The U.S. government budget deficit remains stubbornly elevated, hovering just north of 6% of gross domestic product (GDP), and the debt-to-GDP ratio is tracking beyond 100%.1 The U.S. Federal Reserve's policy tools may be stretched thin: U.S. government debt interest expenses now exceed $1 trillion annually,2 inflation is still resting at ~3%,3 and market "what if" scenarios, like debt spiral or reserve diversification, are at least getting discussed more widely.

Gold: A Strategic Comeback

Gold isn't just riding a wave; it's leading one. Spot gold is now trading in the $3,600–$3,700 per ounce range, recently hitting intraday highs of above $3,700.4

Meanwhile, central banks have been in a gold accumulation mode. They purchased approximately 244 tonnes in Q1 and 166 tonnes in Q2, bringing total first-half net acquisitions to 410 tonnes, the strongest mid-year tally in years.5 Across 2025, Metals Focus projects total gold purchases to reach 1,000 tonnes, marking a fourth consecutive year of massive institutional buying.6

The story is well known, but still powerful: Gold doesn't default, it doesn't inflate away, and in a world testing the limits of sovereign liability, it could be among the most credible insurance policies on the table.

Bitcoin: The New Monetary Challenger

Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) presence in the conversation isn't accidental. With a circulating supply mathematically capped at 21 million coins,7 Bitcoin introduces a form of digital scarcity the world has never had before.8 Its network secures more than $2.1 trillion in market value, processing billions of dollars in transactions daily with no central authority.9 Unlike gold, which has a deep past, Bitcoin has a fast-moving present: It's programmable, it settles across borders in minutes, and it's attracting the interest of both sovereign entities and institutional allocators. In 2025, we're not just watching Bitcoin mature into a parallel monetary asset; we're watching it evolve into infrastructure for a new financial architecture. If gold is the archetype of enduring value, Bitcoin is the prototype of monetary innovation, and that juxtaposition is what makes the comparison so compelling right now.

Washington Pushes for Digital Asset Leadership

2025 has marked a decisive pivot in Washington's approach to crypto. Rather than sitting back, the Trump administration has begun to actively position the U.S. as the world's hub for digital financial technology. The policy goals are clear: protect investors, incentivize builders to stay onshore and ensure U.S. capital markets remain the deepest and most innovative. What had once been a regulatory patchwork is now giving way to a coordinated framework designed to bring digital assets into the mainstream of American finance.

From Stablecoins to Market Structure: The New Framework

The summer's legislative activity showed just how quickly the agenda is moving. The GENIUS Act, signed in early July, established the first comprehensive federal framework for stablecoins, requiring issuers to hold full reserves in dollars or Treasuries, effectively hard-wiring stablecoins into U.S. monetary policy.10 The House also passed the CLARITY Act, now awaiting Senate action, which would assign primary oversight of digital commodities to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and end years of legal uncertainty.11 Alongside these moves, the House approved the Anti-CBDC12 Surveillance State Act, signaling a preference for private stablecoins over a government-issued retail CBDC.13 Taken together, these steps amount to a turning point: instead of ambiguity and enforcement by litigation, the U.S. is finally sketching a blueprint for how the crypto economy should operate.

Retirement Accounts and Wall Street's Embrace

Potentially, the boldest stroke came in August, when President Trump signed an executive order directing the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to make room for crypto and other alternatives inside 401(k) and defined contribution plans.14 With approximately $43 trillion in U.S. retirement assets,15 even a sliver allocated to digital assets could transform adoption dynamics.

Defining What We Mean by "Risk"

Before diving into the numbers, it's worth pausing on what we mean by "risk." Many investors instinctively hear the word and think of losses or volatility, but risk is not a fixed constant; it is contextual. A pension fund, a retail saver and a venture investor may all look at the same asset and perceive very different risks. For some, it's about the possibility of losing capital. For others, it's about volatility relative to their liabilities or benchmarks. And for many, it's about the chance that an asset simply fails to deliver when needed. In this piece, when we talk about risk, we're narrowing in on measurable price variability, specifically, volatility. Volatility is not a perfect definition of risk, but it is one of the most accessible ways to compare assets across time and across categories.

Rolling Volatility: A Common Yardstick

To make the comparison meaningful, we'll use rolling three-month and six-month volatility windows in figures 1a and 1b, respectively. This approach smooths out the noise of day-to-day swings and allows us to see whether risk is trending up, trending down or holding steady. And while Bitcoin is often labeled "digital gold," it's important to note that its comparator, gold, is far from risk-free. Over the past few decades, gold has regularly exhibited double-digit annualized volatility, higher than many investors assume for a monetary asset that has been trusted for thousands of years. By looking at rolling volatility for both Bitcoin and gold, we can place the common narrative of "Bitcoin as high risk" into proper context. What emerges is not a binary story of risky versus safe, but a spectrum where even the oldest monetary asset carries more variability than its reputation might suggest.

  • Bitcoin's volatility reflects the macro shocks of the past five years. Look at the peaks in 2020–2021: they coincide with COVID-19's liquidity crisis and the surge of speculative inflows during the "easy money" era. Volatility spiked again around 2022 as inflation roared, the Fed tightened aggressively, and high-profile crypto failures (Luna, FTX) shook confidence. Since then, Bitcoin's swings have moderated, with 2024–2025 volatility compressing into the 30%–50% range, still high, but clearly trending toward greater stability as institutional adoption deepens.
  • Gold remains calmer, but not static. While its three-month volatility mostly sits between 10% and 20%, the chart shows moments of uptick, such as in 2020 when investors scrambled for liquidity, and again in late 2024–early 2025 as geopolitical tensions and central bank buying pushed gold above $3,700/oz. The message is clear: gold may be centuries old, but even it reacts sharply to stress. The contrast is not "safe vs. risky," but degrees of movement under pressure, with Bitcoin beginning to look less like chaos and more like a higher-beta cousin of gold.

Figure 1a:Volatility in Context: Bitcoin vs. Gold (Three-Month Annualized Volatility)

  • Bitcoin's volatility smooths but still tells the macro story. Using a six-month lens, the extreme spikes of the three-month chart are muted, but the same episodes stand out: COVID-19 liquidity shocks in 2020, the 2021 boom and bust and the 2022 tightening cycle with crypto-specific failures. What changes is the perspective: instead of sudden bursts, the six-month series shows a clear arc of declining baseline volatility, from the 70%–100% range in 2020–21 down toward the 40%–50% range by 2024–25. That's the key insight: as adoption has broadened, Bitcoin's volatility profile has been compressing on a structural level.
  • Gold is steadier, but the six-month view highlights subtle shifts. The longer window shows gold almost always sitting in the 10%–20% band, but with gentle rises around global stress points, 2020's dash for cash and the renewed geopolitical and central-bank buying cycle of 2024–25. What the six-month comparison really shows is scale: Gold's volatility changes, but it does so in smaller amplitude; Bitcoin's changes are larger but trending downward. The combination of the three- and six-month views gives investors both the immediacy of short-term shocks and the reassurance of longer-term convergence.

Figure 1b: Zooming out: Risk Looks Different at Six Months (Six-Month Annualized Volatility)

From Perception to Measured Reality

For years, Bitcoin has carried the label of being "too risky," a caricature shaped by its volatile episodes and youth as an asset class. But looking at rolling volatility next to gold reframes that story. Yes, Bitcoin moves more, but the distance between the two assets is narrowing, and the trajectory is toward stabilization. Gold, meanwhile, is not nearly as placid as its reputation suggests; it too fluctuates in ways that matter to investors who prize capital preservation. Risk, as our analysis shows, is not a binary divide between "safe" and "dangerous." It is a spectrum, shifting with time and circumstance.

Why This Matters for 2025 and Beyond

The point isn't that Bitcoin and gold are interchangeable. It's that when investors think about protecting themselves from fiscal stress, dollar fragility or geopolitical shocks, both assets deserve a place in the conversation. Gold offers historical credibility and institutional comfort; Bitcoin offers innovation, programmability and digital scarcity. The key insight is that their risks may not be as far apart as the headlines suggest. For investors trying to navigate an uncertain 2025, the better question is not whether Bitcoin is "too risky," but how its evolving profile compares to gold, and could combining both offer a more resilient hedge against the dollar's unresolved challenges.

Citations

1 Source: "The Budget and Economic Outlook: 2025 to 2035," U.S. Congressional Budget Office, 2025.

2 Source: "Interest costs on the national debt: Monthly interest tracker" [data through July 2025], in Programs and Projects: Fiscal Policy by Peter G. Peterson Foundation, U.S. Bureau of the Fiscal Service, 2025.

3 Source: "Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U): U.S. city average, all items, July 2025" [news release], U.S. Department of Labor, U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 8/12/25.

4 Source: Gold price per ounce, Bullion.com, 9/18/25.

5 Source: "Gold demand trends: Q1 2025 – Central bank demand" [data set], World Gold Council, 2025.

6 Source: P. Devitt, "Central banks on track for fourth year of massive gold purchases, Metals Focus says," Reuters, 6/5/25.

7 The final fraction of a Bitcoin is expected to be mined around the year 2140, when the supply hits 21 million coins.

8 Source: S. Nakamoto, "Bitcoin: A peer-to-peer electronic cash system," 2008.

9 Source: "Bitcoin (BTC) price and market statistics as of August 29, 2025," MidForex, 8/29/25.

10 Source: The GENIUS Act (stablecoins legislation, signed into law by President Trump in July 2025), U.S. Congress, 7/25, Public Law No: 118-XXX.

11 Source: The CLARITY Act (market structure bill, passed House, pending Senate), U.S. House of Representatives, 7/25, H.R. XXXX, 118th Congress.

12 CBDC stands for central bank digital currency.

data.title: monday.com: AI - From Headwind To Tailwind
data.description:
The AI Divide - Infrastructure vs. Application Software Providers

monday.com (NASDAQ:MNDY) shares fell nearly 20% after reporting second-quarter results on August 11, 2025, despite strong growth in both revenue and earnings. Revenue rose 27% year-over-year to $299 million, while non-GAAP EPS increased 16% to $1.09. Both figures beat analyst estimates. The sharp decline was driven by management's modest raise to 2025 guidance, which failed to match the strength of the Q2 beat and left investors with a muted outlook. Concerns were compounded by weaker organic search traffic, a risk to the company's self-serve acquisition model.

monday.com's slump fits a broader pattern across the software sector. While stock prices of software companies that are providing the infrastructure to run AI have soared this year (MSFT +23%, GOOG +34%, ORCL +85%) application providers have suffered. MNDY's stock price is down roughly 7% year-to-date, in line with other application software companies such as GITL (-11%), CRM (-26%), and ADBE (-18%).

The weakness in software shares is often attributed to fears that artificial intelligence will disrupt the industry. The concern is that companies could use AI to build internal tools or that startups will displace incumbents. In my view, this narrative overlooks the difficulty of developing, maintaining, and scaling in-house solutions. It also underestimates the advantages of established vendors' long-standing client relationships and enterprise-grade platforms with governance, security, and compliance features that earn customer trust. This is supported by a recent MIT study, "The Gen AI Divide - State of Business in 2025." The research study found that internally developed AI solutions fail twice as often as externally sourced ones. It also highlighted a strong preference among companies to rely on existing vendors when implementing new AI tools.

On the other hand, massive investment in AI infrastructure will ultimately drive down the cost of compute. Hence, software companies that harness AI could become some of the earliest and strongest beneficiaries of the technology, and among the first to monetize it at scale.

Insights from monday.com's 2025 Investor Day

Financial outlook

On September 17, 2025, MNDY hosted an investor day outlining its strategy for the future. Emphasis was on adoption of its newer products like monday CRM, monday System and the company's opportunity to cross sell these across their 250,000 existing customers. Additionally, monday showcased its AI-empowered tools monday magic, monday vibe and monday sidekick, which have just been released and whose usage has increased significantly within a very short time period.

Management set a revenue target of $1.8 billion for fiscal year 2027 and a long-term adjusted operating margin of 20-25%. The market's initial reaction was muted, with the shares falling about 4% on the day. The guidance was broadly in line with Street expectations, and some investors may have hoped for a higher number and/or for a faster trajectory in operating margin improvement. MNDY emphasised, however, that it remains firmly in an investment phase: headcount is expected to increase by 30% in FY2025 and by a further 20% in FY2026. While elevated compute expenses will weigh on margins in the near term. At the same time, management has historically taken a very conservative approach when issuing revenue guidance, it beat EPS and revenue estimates in all of its last 17 quarterly releases:

Cross-selling potential

With only 6% of customers using more than one monday product, there are substantial cross-selling opportunities that the company is looking to monetize. The growth rate of monday's new product suite outside of its core workflow management tool looks promising, growing 98% year over year but so far only representing 9.4% of the company's total sales.

New products are scaling rapidly, growing faster than monday's original workflow product. monday CRM has now reached $100 million in annual recurring revenue. The newly launched monday Service is growing at the fastest rate of any monday.com product. The company originally forecast that new products would make up a low double-digit percentage of total sales by the end of fiscal year 2025. During the investor day, management indicated that this target will be clearly surpassed.

Furthermore, monday is looking to change its pricing model. Currently, it charges customers solely based on the number of seats. Going forward, it will adopt a hybrid pricing approach: the existing products will still be priced per seat, the new AI features will be charged based on consumption. This consumption-based pricing for the AI features will be introduced during FY 2026. Management has stated clearly that their 2027 revenue target does not factor in any contribution from the AI tools. This is because they first need to understand how consumption will develop once consumption pricing starts. However, so far, usage of the AI features has been very strong.

From this, I assume that the $1.8 billion 2027 revenue target carries significant upside potential, including for profitability. The market appears to share this view. Although the shares dropped on the day of the investor day, they performed strongly in the following days. After the initial reaction, the stock climbed 9.4% and then 6.3% over the subsequent two days. This rebound came as the market digested the investor day information and analysts returned with their feedback.

Valuation

For valuing the shares, I focus on the enterprise value to sales (EV/sales) multiple. Although the company is already profitable and is expected to generate well over $300 million in free cash flow this year, it remains in a heavy investment phase. Due to this significant ongoing investment, the EV/sales multiple better reflects the current valuation than profit-based multiples.

Over the last three years, monday has traded at an average of around 10x EV/Sales, while on FY2025 estimates, the multiple has compressed to roughly 7.9x lately.

To check how this compares to the broader software sector, I have run a data query comparing a set of 30 software companies and plotted their current year expected revenue growth rate vs. their current EV/sales multiple. As can be seen from the plot with 26% revenue growth and a multiple of around 7.9x, MNDY is trading at an attractive valuation compared to the overall universe.

The median 2025 revenue growth of the sample is 15.1%, while the median EV/sales multiple is 9.0x. Of course, valuation differences can be explained by sector attributes, profitability, or other company-specific factors. Even so, based on its growth prospects, I believe Monday deserves to be valued at a 10x EV/sales multiple. This is in line with its historic trading levels and still attractively priced compared to the broader peer group.

The company's $1.8 billion revenue target for 2027 implies a compound annual growth rate of 21.5% between 2025 and 2027. I believe that with some additional tailwind from AI monetization, monday.com should be able to sustain revenue growth at a higher level, in line with its expected 2025 growth rate of more than 25%. This would translate to expected revenues of approximately $1.53 billion for FY2026. Based on this sales estimate and after adjusting for cash on the balance sheet, the target share price over the next 12 months comes to about $319. This price target represents a roughly 45% upside compared to the share price of around $219 as of writing this article.

Risks

From my perspective, a key risk for monday.com's share price is that the company may struggle to sustain its high-growth trajectory as its further expansion into adjacent offerings and new customers stall. While the company is a leader in workflow management tools, it still needs to scale its other products. On investor day, the management confirmed that it is not yet using monday CRM internally; hence, to make this a viable alternative to the incumbent providers, it needs to prove that the product can run on a large scale.

At the same time, the company has heavily invested in its AI offerings. It stressed several times on its investor day that "it is going all in on AI". If customers prove reluctant to pay for the AI-powered tools, the company could find itself unable to monetise those investments. Hence, slowing product adoption or weaker AI monetisation would cast doubt on the durability of the growth story.

Additionally, the company has reached positive operating margins, but it is still investing heavily in sales, R&D, new products, and new markets. These costs may delay further margin improvement, and if growth slows, fixed expenses could weigh on profitability. Another concern is customer acquisition. Specifically for its small and medium customers, monday has long relied on leads from Google searches, but its latest results show this channel has weakened. While the company is expanding into other target groups like the enterprise market and pursuing alternative marketing avenues, any inefficiency in bringing in new users could push up costs and dampen growth.

Final thoughts

MNDY has huge cross-selling potential with a base of 250,000 customers, of which only 6% are using more than one monday product and its new product offerings monday CRM and monday Systems are scaling fast. Its newest revenue guide does not include any contribution from the monetisation of its AI tools and its valuation is attractive for its growth and margin potential. Rather than a threat to its business, AI could accelerate the company' business going forward. Buy.

data.title: Time To Hunt Bargains In The BDC Forest: 2 Picks At ~20% Discount To NAV
data.description:
My closest followers have most probably noticed that while I am long and bullish on certain BDCs, when it comes to the broader sector perspectives, then my sentiment is quite bearish, to say the least.

So far, I have been focused on BDC dividends and their sustainability levels. The current market dynamics in combination with the state of "limited margin of safety" at which most BDCs are operating at, in my view, creates an excellent set-up for seeing system-wide dividend cuts.

I will not repeat myself, but, in a nutshell, things boil down to this:

  • Sector average base dividend coverage of 102% (on Q2 data).
  • Recent interest rate cuts that will negatively impact Q3 and Q4 2025 financials.
  • Potentially (actually, very likely) new interest rate cuts on the table soon.
  • No clear data that would prove that the sector-wide spread compression has stopped.
  • Sector average debt-to-equity of 1.19x, which means that many BDCs do not have incremental leverage capacity to offset any of these headwinds.

This is bad, and I have not changed my views here at all.

Having said that, it is not that BDCs (BIZD) have lived in a vacuum. The market has recognized these issues and priced the sector accordingly. For example, the sector median P/NAV is 0.88x. So, most BDCs trade at double digit discounts.

And let's keep in mind that these are averages.

There are certain securities that are still relatively richly priced such as Main Street Capital (MAIN) and Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) - trading at P/NAV of 2.01x and 1.37x, respectively. As much as I like MAIN and TSLX, it is hard to justify paying such a high price, while most of the sector is in a discount territory and, importantly, the headwinds that I outlined above are indeed quite negative.

Similarly, there are some deep-value BDCs that trade at even steeper discounts than ~12%. Granted, most of them are very speculative and deserve to be priced so. Think of FSK KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) and TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC Corp. (TPVG) - all with consistent problems on the non-accrual end.

In this heavily discounted area, it could actually be worth spending a bit of time to potentially scoop up bargains. I know that even if we succeed in finding some unfairly punished cases, the chances of them being truly sustainable income producers remain extremely limited. Yet, it does not mean that on a total return basis, the investments would be unattractive. The steeper the discount, the bigger price return potential (through price-to-value convergence).

Let me present you two heavily discounted BDC picks, which even though are not my top BDC income choices (due to lack of significant margin of safety on the dividend coverage end), they certainly carry very attractive total return prospects.

#1: Carlyle Secured Lending- 18% below NAV

Carlyle Secured Lending Inc. (CGBD) is a typical BDC, which focuses on first-lien loans in the $25+ million EBITDA company segment. It is not a venture capital biased one nor a vehicle that goes heavy on equity co-investments such as Main Street Capital and Fidus Investment (FDUS). There is also no exposure to CLOs or aggressive loan investment structures.

Namely, we can make a safe argument that CGBD is an average/standard BDC with no specific nuances that might justify abnormal valuations.

So, the question is then why CGBD trades at almost 10% discount to already discounted BDC peer base. Frankly, I do not see a reason for that. In fact, I would even argue that CGBD should deserve slight premium over the sector median, which has a P/NAV of 0.88x. Now, I am not saying that CGBD has the necessary properties for being priced at NAV (not even speaking about premium), but something closer to, say, Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) could be justified.

I will give you three main reasons.

First, CGBD has already managed to stabilize its NII per share generation by registering only $0.01 per share decline relative to the Q1 2025 period. Here it is important to mention that the portfolio weighted average debt yield level has remained unchanged in Q2 at 10.9%, which is largely in line with the prevailing sector average levels. In other words, CGBD does not have a yield level from which there could be a meaningful fall.

Second, speaking of NII per share stability, it is clear that CGBD is exposed to some major headwinds (most importantly, the lowering of interesting rates). Even though I doubt that CGBD will manage to sustain the current NII per share levels, it has at least a slight (relative) advantage of having a spare debt capacity on its books that could be used to fund portfolio growth. As of Q2 2025, CGBD's debt to equity stood at 1.1x, while the sector average is closer to 1.2x.

Third, and this is also something we can consider a relative advantage, CGBD has all of its external leverage loans tied to SOFR. What this means is that compared to most other peers, which carry some exposure to fixed rate debt, CGBD should be able to maximize the benefit of reduced financing costs (albeit, on a net basis, the effects are negative).

Now, we could theoretically make a case that CGBD deserves a higher discount than the sector median because of the non-accrual rate, which ending Q2 was at 2.1% (of FV). Yet, the thing is that right after Q2 2025, CGBD managed to restructure the struggling loan case, thereby bringing down the non-accrual rate to only 1.0%, which is definitely below sector average.

Another issue, which is the main reason for me to not become super bullish on CGBD, is the base dividend coverage of 98%. It means that the current dividend is underfunded; hence the dividend cut risk is high.

Yet, let me put this in the following context:

  • Currently, CGBD's yield is 12.6%. Assuming a ~25% dividend cut, the yield would still remain quite enticing and actually in line with the sector average if we carve out such extremes as Prospect Capital (PSEC), Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and Oxford Square Capital Corp. (OXSQ) - these yield close to 20%.
  • Most of the key fundamental metrics, as described above, are at above average territory in terms of their quality and the embedded margin of safety.

So, I think that it is rather obvious that CGBD has been unfairly punished, at least compared to the rest of the sector. For patient and value-oriented investors, CGBD could be an interesting choice to consider for potential alpha (juicy income and notable price gain potential).

#2: Barings BDC, 17% below NAV

Barings BDC Inc (BBDC) has a very similar story to that of CGBD.

At its core, BBDC is a traditional BDC with a simple business model that is centered around mostly first-lien lending to well cash-flowing enterprises that generate EBITDA in the range of $25 million to $75 million. There is no material exposure to equity products, CLOs or venture capital investments.

So, CGBD is subject to almost exactly the same headwinds as CGBD and the lion's share of the sector. Yet, somehow we see that when it comes to P/NAV, it trades well below the already discounted sector median.

I will start with couple of things that differentiate BBDC from CGBD (in a positive manner).

Based on Q2 2025 data points, the dividend coverage level was 108%, which is better than for CGBD, better than the sector average of 102%, and good for incremental shock absorption, which will inevitably come from lower interest rates. At least from this perspective, the event of BBDC trimming its dividend lies farther in the future than for CGBD. Importantly, the NII per share generation has clearly stabilized in Q2.

Moreover, BBDC's weighted average portfolio debt yield (10.1%) is rather low, at one of the lowest levels in the sector, which is, obviously, a notable positive in terms of facing lighter consequences from spread compression. In addition, we have to appreciate BBDC's solid track-record on the non-accruals. Historically, it has managed to keep them well below sector average, at around 1.0% of FV (this includes a period like the COVID-19 pandemic). Currently, the non-accrual rate sits at 0.5% - something that most peers cannot match.

Now, the only negative driver that I could find in BBDC's books is the relatively indebted balance sheet, which as of Q2 2025 had a debt to equity of 1.33x. It is more than clear that from this kind of level it is almost impossible to secure incremental portfolio growth without becoming overly aggressive. My expectation is that BBDC slowly but surely works off the leverage to a more balanced level of around 1.15x to 1.25x, which is actually what has been the BDC's long-term average. This process is inherently negative for the NII per share generation, especially in the context of forthcoming pressures.

Having said that, we have to always put all of the variables together and assess them collectively to see whether the entire equation leads indeed to a justified valuation (in this case, a massive discount).

In my humble opinion, there is no substantiated reason behind why BBDC should trade at this kind of discount to the sector median.

So, I would say that BBDC is one of the few BDC bargains out there that offers high dividends in combination with strong upside potential.

data.title: Virtus Westchester Event-Driven Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

In the first half of 2025, the landscape for mergers and acquisitions (M&A) and corporate reorganizations showed significant momentum. Dealmakers have been active, despite the complex macro environment, which included elevated interest rates, tariff concerns, and regulatory uncertainties. Global M&A activity surged 30% versus this time last year. Year-to-date transaction volume reached $2.2 trillion as of June 30, signaling renewed corporate confidence and expectations for clarity around regulations.

The final two months of the second quarter were particularly strong, despite challenges created by President Trump’s proposed new tariffs. However, resilient markets and easing antitrust enforcement spurred activity, with multiple large deals expected in the latter half of 2025.

The U.S.’s $1.2 trillion total dominated global M&A activity. Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) tallied $659 billion, up 12% year-over-year, and the Asia-Pacific region (APAC), particularly Japan, recorded its second-highest deal volume ever. This global surge was supported by a more deal-friendly regulatory environment and easing trade tensions.

Corporate reorganizations, such as spinoffs and split-offs, generated $237 billion in volume this year. Sectors such as energy and technology (focused on AI and cybersecurity) are leading this trend. We look for transactions with strong fundamentals and strategic alignment to maximize the odds of transaction success. Financial sponsors, such as private equity firms, drove $456 billion in transactions year-to-date, for 38% of the total M&A volume.

Performance

The Virtus Westchester Event-Driven Fund I-shares (MUTF:WCEIX)(Fund) returned 2.31% in the second quarter, bringing year-to-date performance to 4.43%. Reflecting an almost 4:1 ratio of winners to losers, 59 positions posted positive gains, while 16 had negative marks-to-market. There were no broken transactions. In addition, 15 deals were completed, with capital redeployed into 17 new situations during the quarter. The subcategories of arbitrage and opportunistic credit recorded positive gains, contributing 1.95% and 0.69%, respectively. Special situations and restructurings experienced mark-to-market losses of -0.22% and -0.11%, respectively, in the quarter. As of the end of June, we held 48 positions and were approximately 125% invested.

Positive Contributors

United States Steel Corp./Nippon Steel Corp. (OTCPK:NPSCY)(63 basis points [bps]): The $14.9 billion acquisition of U.S. Steel by Nippon Steel, finalized on June 18, 2025, overcame significant regulatory and political hurdles. Initially blocked by President Biden, the Trump administration secured the U.S. government a “golden share,” commitments to $11 billion in investments, and 100,000 jobs, which paved the way for its eventual approval. As one of the few arbitrage funds that remained committed to this opportunity, our patience and strategic conviction paid off, delivering strong value as the deal closed.

Discover Financial Services/Capital One Financial Corp. (COF)(32 bps): The $35.3 billion all-stock acquisition of Discover Financial Services by Capital One Financial Corporation, finalized on May 18, 2025, was our second-largest contributor to portfolio performance in 2Q. The deal created the largest U.S. credit card issuer by loan volume, and the sixth-largest bank by assets. Our position capitalized on the deal’s attractive spread, delivering strong risk-adjusted returns as the transaction closed.

Mauser Packaging Solutions (31 bps): We originally purchased the company’s 7.25% 2025 bonds several years ago, anticipating a sale to another private equity firm. That deal fell through, and in 2023, Mauser exchanged these bonds for 9.25% 2027 bonds with a higher coupon and longer maturity, plus a fee. Spreads stabilized throughout the quarter and the bonds recovered toward their historical trading range.

Performance Detractors

Becton, Dickinson & Company (BDX)(-38 bps): In April 2025, the company announced its plan to spin off its Biosciences and Diagnostic Solutions business, targeting completion in fiscal 2026. In 2Q, the position incurred a mark-to-market loss driven by mixed financial results in the Biosciences unit, which reported weaker-than-expected margins, broader market volatility, and analyst downgrades citing reduced research funding in China and the U.S., a U.S. government ban on certain exports to China, and tariff uncertainties.

Macro Portfolio Hedge (-27 bps): Our macro portfolio hedge, designed to mitigate downside risk, provided a buffer during the April market sell-off following President Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcements. However, the subsequent market rallies in May and June reversed these gains, turning the hedge into a detractor.

Kellanova/Mars Inc. (K)(-25 bps): The $35.9 billion acquisition, announced in August 2024, remains a high-conviction investment, despite ongoing regulatory hurdles. The U.S. Federal Trade Commission cleared the deal on June 25, 2025, without conditions, securing 27 of 28 required regulatory approvals. However, the European Commission’s Phase II antitrust investigation delayed the expected closing to late 2025. Despite this, the potential for strong risk-adjusted returns, even with possible divestitures under $750 million, supports our commitment to this transformative transaction.

Conclusion

The remainder of 2025 appears poised for increasing activity, supported by resilient corporate confidence and easing U.S. regulatory conditions, though some challenges remain. Ongoing European regulatory scrutiny, potential tariff-related disruptions, and geopolitical uncertainties could affect the pace of announcements and widen spreads for some transactions—offering opportunities to deploy capital at potentially more attractive levels. Strategic consolidations and spinoffs are likely to remain active, with financial sponsors continuing to deploy capital. Our portfolios are positioned to navigate these dynamics, focusing on transactions with the perceived highest risk-reward profile, and filtering for high-probability opportunities.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet ABS/MBS ETF (VABS) Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Securitized markets were volatile in April as tariff announcements drove spreads 10–15 basis points wider month-over-month. Early-April volatility temporarily stalled new issuance, but supply rebounded sharply once conditions stabilized. In May, stronger-than-expected employment data, widening fiscal deficits, and a partial tariff rollback led to a backup in interest rates. However, spreads on private-label securitized products tightened meaningfully versus Treasuries. After an issuance lull tied to the “Liberation Day” tariff shock, May became the most active new-issue month of the year. Investors responded with increased demand—particularly for subordinate tranches—as they sought to add yield and leverage to portfolios. June brought spread tightening, fueled by robust demand and a surge in primary issuance. With Treasury yields falling, securitized products delivered positive total returns to close out the quarter.

Sector Performance

The Fund has significant exposure to asset-backed securities (ABS)—its allocation to ABS returned 1.68% for the quarter. The U.S. consumer credit fundamentals remained generally healthy in 2Q, though signs of inflation stress emerged. Roughly 11% of consumers were making only minimum credit card payments—the highest in 12 years, per the Philadelphia Federal Reserve. Delinquencies also edged higher among both prime and subprime borrowers, though ratings agencies continued to upgrade consumer ABS deals thanks to steady deleveraging. That said, jobs data remained solid with 4.2% unemployment, while used car prices held firm, supporting recovery rates on auto loan defaults. While full-year ABS issuance is steady, it trails 2024 levels. Relative to short corporates, ABS looks historically cheap.

The Fund’s other large exposure is non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS)—its allocation to RMBS returned 1.57% for the quarter. By the end of April, RMBS spreads retraced about a third of their tariff-driven widening, and valuations firmed. The recovery in risk/reward brought investors back, and new issue volumes picked up accordingly. Mid-quarter, spreads continued tightening, returning to 2025 averages. Investor interest remained strong, particularly in senior tranches, as spreads continued to offer a compelling premium. June brought stability to RMBS credit conditions. Though delinquencies and bankruptcies rose modestly among lower-rated borrowers over the quarter, their levels remained low historically. National housing dynamics were still favorable, but oversupply signs emerged regionally, especially in Florida and Texas. Overall, investor demand supported firm spreads.

Fund Performance

The Fund returned 1.69% at NAV for the quarter versus the ICE BofA 1-3 Year A-BBB US Corporate Index returns of 1.48%.

Positive Contributors

  • Aircraft lease and subordinate auto loan securitizations were positive contributors for the period.
  • The Fund’s exposure to commercial mortgage-backed securities and RMBS produced strong total returns for the quarter.

Performance Detractors

  • Our position in unsecured consumer loans lagged due to shorter durations.
  • Our position in timeshare loans lagged for the quarter as the asset tends to underperform when the yield curve steepens.
